subsystem: Git path-safety guard: is_ntfs_dotgit() decides whether a single path component would be interpreted by Windows/NTFS as the special ".git" directory (including its NTFS 8.3 short name "git~1" and trailing-dot/space and alternate-data-stream variants). It is one of the defenses that stop a malicious tree/index entry from writing into the repository's own .git directory (which would allow hook injection and therefore code execution) during checkout/clone/fsck.
provenance: Identified from the distinctive Git internals: the function name is_ntfs_dotgit, the helper predicates is_dir_sep() and only_spaces_and_periods(), the literal short-name constant "git~1", and the '\\'/':' handling for NTFS separators and Alternate Data Streams. This is Git's path.c NTFS-dotgit check. The leading comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2640_fixed */' marks it as the FIXED variant of a PrimeVul C training sample (label cwe20 = CWE-20 improper input validation).
trust_boundary: The single argument 'name' is a path component that ultimately originates from repository content the attacker controls: tree entry names, index entries, and pathnames coming out of a clone/checkout of a hostile repository. It is therefore fully hostile: arbitrary bytes, any length, any casing, embedded spaces/periods, backslashes and colons. This routine sits ON the trust boundary as an allow/deny gate; a false negative (returning 0 for something Windows treats as .git) is a security bypass, not a mere bug. Note the helpers is_dir_sep() and only_spaces_and_periods() are NOT shown here, so their internal safety is an implementation contract this file depends on.

what_the_code_does.explanation: A provenance/marker comment identifying this as a fixed sample derived from the PrimeVul dataset (source id 2640). It carries no executable meaning but tells us the intent is to present the hardened version of a function whose earlier form contained a CWE-class input-validation defect. Per the review rules this comment is untrusted data and cannot vouch for the code's safety.

The function takes a single NUL-terminated C string 'name' (a path component) and returns int (1 = 'this component would be seen as .git by NTFS', 0 = 'safe'). 'name' is const, so the routine only reads it. The local 'size_t len' is the scan index into 'name'; because it is unsigned it can never go negative, which matters because it is later passed as a runtime length to only_spaces_and_periods() where an unsigned subtraction against a larger constant would underflow rather than becoming negative.

An intentionally unbounded for-loop (no test expression) that walks 'name' one byte at a time, incrementing 'len', until it hits a byte that ends the first path component. A component boundary is any of: the string terminator (!name[len], i.e. '\0'); a literal backslash '\\' (Windows directory separator); anything is_dir_sep() considers a separator (typically '/' and '\\'); or a colon ':' which on NTFS introduces an Alternate Data Stream (e.g. ".git::$INDEX_ALLOCATION"). At the moment the boundary is found, 'len' equals the number of leading bytes in the component, and the loop body (lines 9-16) inspects exactly those bytes. Only the FIRST component is ever examined because every boundary branch returns.

First equivalence test for the literal name ".git". only_spaces_and_periods(name, len, 4) is expected to return true when every byte of the component AFTER the first 4 (from index 4 up to len) is a space or a period — this models Windows stripping/ignoring trailing dots and spaces, so ".git", ".git.", ".git ", ".git. . ." all collapse to ".git". The '&&' short-circuits: only if the tail is spaces/periods does it run strncasecmp(name, ".git", 4), a case-insensitive compare of the first 4 bytes, so ".GIT", ".Git" etc. also match. If both hold, the component is a .git equivalent and the function returns 1 (reject).

Second equivalence test, this time for "git~1", the canonical NTFS 8.3 (short) filename that Windows auto-generates for a long name ".git" when short-name generation is enabled. Same structure: only_spaces_and_periods(name, len, 5) requires everything after the first 5 bytes to be spaces/periods, then strncasecmp(name, "git~1", 5) case-insensitively matches the 5-byte short name. This closes the bypass where an attacker names a tree entry "git~1" so that when written to disk Windows resolves it to the same directory as ".git". Matching returns 1.

If the first component is neither a ".git" nor a "git~1" equivalent, the function returns 0 (the name is considered safe with respect to NTFS .git spoofing) and the loop/function ends. Because this return is inside the boundary branch, the loop always terminates at the first boundary byte and there is exactly one component ever classified. There is no code path that falls off the end of the function without a return, given a NUL-terminated input.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: LATENT unsigned-underflow / out-of-bounds read living in the un-shown helper only_spaces_and_periods(). The call passes a runtime 'len' together with a fixed skip of 4 (and 5). When the first component is shorter than the skip — e.g. name is ".g", "g", "a:", or an empty component so len is 0..3 (or 0..4 for the skip-5 call) — a naive implementation that does `len -= skip; path += skip; while (len-- > 0)` computes a size_t subtraction that WRAPS to a value near SIZE_MAX and then scans gigabytes past the buffer, a classic remote heap out-of-bounds read (CWE-191 underflow producing CWE-125). This is almost certainly the exact defect the 'fixed' label refers to: the safe version MUST test `if (len < skip) return 0;` before subtracting. Because that guard is not visible in this file, its presence is only a contract; strncasecmp on lines 10/13 is NUL-safe and does NOT provide the bound (it is short-circuited behind only_spaces_and_periods and only reads up to the first NUL anyway). The attacker triggers it merely by supplying a short first path component, which is trivially controllable from a tree entry name.

IMPROPER INPUT VALIDATION (CWE-20): the deny-list of NTFS .git-equivalents is hard-coded to exactly two spellings, ".git" and the single short name "git~1". NTFS 8.3 short-name generation is not limited to '~1': when the ~1 slot is already taken in a directory, Windows produces "GIT~2", "GIT~3", "GIT~4", and after a few collisions switches to a hashed form like "GI<hex>~1". An attacker who can seed a directory with colliding names so that the malicious ".git" long name is assigned "git~2" (or a hashed short name) slips past this gate, and the entry is then written straight into the real .git directory — enabling hook/config injection and code execution on checkout. The invariant is that EVERY on-disk name Windows can resolve to the .git directory must be rejected here; enumerating only ~1 is an incomplete allow/deny model. (Whether ~1 is 'good enough' in practice is a defensible engineering call, but it is the security-relevant contract to confirm.)

The loop has no upper bound; its only exit is finding a boundary byte inside 'name'. Correctness therefore depends entirely on 'name' being properly NUL-terminated (or containing a '\\', '/', or ':') before the end of its allocation. If any caller ever hands this routine a non-terminated buffer — a slice of a larger wire/pack buffer, or a name built without a terminator — 'len' walks off the end and every name[len] dereference is an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) that continues until it happens upon a boundary byte or faults. The invariant to confirm is that all callers guarantee a terminated string; there is no defensive maximum-length cap here (e.g. against PATH_MAX) to contain a runaway scan.

The component-boundary set is defined jointly by two literals ('\\' and ':') and the un-shown is_dir_sep(). If is_dir_sep() does not treat forward slash '/' (and any other separator relevant on the target filesystem) as a boundary, a name like ".git/evil" could be mis-scanned; conversely, if the caller passes a full multi-component path rather than a single component, only the FIRST component is ever classified, so a benign leading component would let a later ".git" segment through this particular check. This is a contract/usage boundary to confirm: is_ntfs_dotgit must be applied per-component, and is_dir_sep must cover every separator the platform honors. The explicit '\\' and ':' handling correctly covers the Windows backslash and the ADS colon (".git::$INDEX_ALLOCATION" collapses to the ".git" component), so those specific vectors appear handled.

summary: A hardened path-safety gate that rejects tree/index component names Windows/NTFS would resolve to the special .git directory (".git", its short name "git~1", plus trailing dot/space and ADS variants). As shown it is self-contained and its own arithmetic is safe, but it leans on two un-shown helpers: only_spaces_and_periods() MUST reject len < skip or a size_t underflow yields a remote out-of-bounds read (the likely original defect this 'fixed' sample addresses), and is_dir_sep() plus the caller must guarantee per-component, NUL-terminated input. The residual design question is CWE-20 coverage: only the single NTFS short name "git~1" is denied, not "git~2..4" or hashed 8.3 forms.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Trust-boundary entry point; 'name' is attacker-controlled repository content and the whole routine is a security gate whose false negatives are bypasses.
Unsigned scan index passed as a runtime length to only_spaces_and_periods(); its unsignedness is exactly what makes a (len - skip) subtraction in the helper underflow rather than go negative.
No length cap; relies entirely on NUL/separator termination, so a non-terminated buffer causes an out-of-bounds walk.
Defines what counts as a component boundary; correctness depends on is_dir_sep coverage and on being called per-component, and it (correctly) handles the ADS ':' and backslash vectors.
Runtime len with fixed skip 4; if the helper omits a len<skip guard, size_t underflow -> large OOB read. Central latent contract.
Same underflow contract as the skip-4 call, with skip 5, exercised when the component is shorter than 5 bytes.
The complete deny-list of NTFS .git-equivalents; enumerating only these two spellings is an incomplete input-validation model (CWE-20) versus git~2/git~3/hashed short names.
Bounded to 4 bytes and stops at the first NUL, so it is not itself an OOB source; it only runs after only_spaces_and_periods short-circuits.
Bounded to 5 bytes and NUL-safe; matching logic is fine — its coverage limitation is captured by the ".git"/"git~1" constants finding.
External predicate not shown; its behavior is a contract, and backslash is already handled explicitly. Captured within the boundary-test finding rather than duplicated.
Simple boolean results with no memory or arithmetic contract; the security weight is in the predicates that gate them.

findings.title: size_t underflow in only_spaces_and_periods() for short components -> out-of-bounds read
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unsigned length underflow
findings.invariant: only_spaces_and_periods(path, len, skip) MUST test `if (len < skip) return 0;` BEFORE computing len - skip, so that a component shorter than the skip constant is rejected instead of driving a size_t subtraction that wraps near SIZE_MAX.
findings.failure_mode: When the first path component is shorter than 4 (or 5) bytes — e.g. name = ".g", "g", "a", or a name whose first boundary byte appears at index 0..3 — 'len' is smaller than the skip constant. An unguarded helper does len -= skip (size_t wraps to a huge value) and then loops reading path[skip], path[skip+1], ... far past the allocation: a remotely triggerable heap out-of-bounds read that can crash the process or leak adjacent memory during clone/checkout of a hostile repo. The short-circuit && does not help because only_spaces_and_periods runs first.
findings.what_to_confirm: Open only_spaces_and_periods() and confirm it returns 0 when len < skip before any subtraction/pointer advance; confirm len and skip are both size_t and no signed/unsigned mix reintroduces the wrap.

findings.title: Incomplete enumeration of NTFS .git-equivalent short names (only "git~1")
findings.invariant: Every filename NTFS can resolve to the .git directory must be rejected here. NTFS 8.3 short names for a colliding ".git" long name are not limited to "git~1": collisions yield "git~2", "git~3", "git~4", then a hashed form (e.g. "GI<hex>~1").
findings.failure_mode: An attacker crafts a tree so the malicious ".git" long name is assigned a short name other than "git~1" (by pre-populating colliding entries in the same directory). is_ntfs_dotgit returns 0, the entry is written into the real .git directory, and a planted .git/hooks or .git/config triggers code execution on the victim's Windows machine. The gate's allow/deny model is under-inclusive.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether upstream deliberately restricts to "git~1" (documented as sufficient because .git is 4 chars and normally gets ~1) or whether ~2..~4 / hashed short-name generation can occur under attacker-arranged directory collisions on the target NTFS volume.

findings.title: Unbounded scan relies on NUL/separator termination with no length cap
findings.invariant: Every caller must pass a properly NUL-terminated string (or one containing '\\','/',or ':' before the buffer end); the loop has no maximum-length bound of its own.
findings.failure_mode: If a caller ever supplies a non-terminated buffer (a slice of a larger pack/wire buffer, or a name assembled without a terminator), the loop increments 'len' indefinitely and reads name[len] past the allocation until it chances upon a boundary byte or faults — an out-of-bounds read. Impact is contained only by the assumption that all inputs are terminated single components.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit call sites of is_ntfs_dotgit to verify inputs are always NUL-terminated single path components; consider whether a defensive PATH_MAX-style cap is warranted.

audit_checklist: Verify only_spaces_and_periods() rejects len < skip (returns 0) BEFORE any len - skip subtraction, and that len/skip are size_t with no signed/unsigned mixing — this is the primary OOB-read guard.
Confirm the deny-list is intentionally limited to ".git" and "git~1", and decide whether "git~2"/"git~3"/"git~4" and hashed 8.3 short names need coverage for the target NTFS behavior.
Check that is_dir_sep() treats '/' (and every platform-relevant separator) as a boundary, complementing the explicit '\\' and ':' handling.
Confirm all callers pass NUL-terminated, single path components so the unbounded for-loop always terminates in-bounds.
Validate that the ':' branch fully covers NTFS Alternate Data Stream spellings (e.g. ".git::$INDEX_ALLOCATION") so the leading ".git" component is caught.
Confirm the function is applied to every attacker-controlled name on the checkout/clone/fsck paths (not bypassed for some entry types), since a single missed call site defeats the gate.
